title: Japchae

overview.description: Korean sweet-potato glass noodles tossed with beef, colourful vegetables and a savoury-sweet soy and sesame dressing.

ingredients: 250g beef steak, such as rump or sirloin, cut into thin strips
method: Mix the beef with 2 tbsp light soy sauce, 1 tsp sugar, 1 tsp sesame oil and the chopped garlic.
Leave to marinate while preparing the other ingredients.
Cook the dangmyeon according to the packet instructions until tender but still pleasantly chewy.
Drain the noodles thoroughly and cut them into shorter lengths with kitchen scissors if desired.
Toss the noodles with the noodle dressing and set aside.
Separate the eggs.
Beat the yolks and whites separately.
Cook the yolk and white separately in a lightly oiled frying pan as thin omelettes.
Allow them to cool, then slice into thin strips.
Stir-fry the onion until softened and lightly golden.
Remove from the pan and set aside.
Stir-fry the carrot until just tender.
Remove and set aside.
Stir-fry the red pepper briefly and set aside.
Stir-fry the mushrooms until softened and lightly browned.
Remove and set aside.
Blanch or wilt the spinach, then squeeze out excess water and season lightly.
Stir-fry the marinated beef until cooked through and lightly browned.
Add the noodles, cooked vegetables, spinach, beef and egg strips to a large pan or bowl.
Toss everything together gently until evenly combined.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Finish with toasted sesame seeds and serve warm or at room temperature.

learning.did-you-know: Japchae originally referred to a mixed vegetable dish created in the early 17th century. Sweet-potato starch noodles were added later and eventually became the defining ingredient of the dish.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to cook and season ingredients separately so each retains its own texture and flavour, then combine them into a balanced finished dish.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A Korean noodle dish with a completely different texture from the egg noodles, udon and rice noodles already in the collection, while also introducing the children to sweet-potato starch noodles.
chapman-tips: Dangmyeon should be chewy rather than soft or mushy.
Cooking the vegetables separately takes longer but gives each ingredient the right texture.
Squeeze excess water from the spinach thoroughly so it doesn't make the finished japchae watery.
The noodles absorb seasoning as they rest, so taste again before serving.
Japchae can be served warm, at room temperature or gently reheated.
For a simpler family version, the vegetables can be cooked together, although the traditional separate cooking method gives the best result.
The beef can be replaced with chicken, pork, prawns, mushrooms or tofu.
notes.batch-cooking: Better fresh, although japchae keeps well in the refrigerator for a few days. Freeze only if necessary, as the noodles can change texture after freezing and reheating.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently in a frying pan with a small splash of water. Japchae is also good cold or at room temperature as part of a packed lunch.
